sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Twelve — Expository and Informational Writing. Today you learn to explain something so clearly that a reader who knows nothing about it walks away understanding it. Three structures. The right transitions. A formal, objective tone — no opinions in costume.

Here is the trap. Last module you learned to argue — claim, evidence, rebuttal. This module sounds similar, but the job is different. Expository writing explains. It does not persuade. The wrong move is to write, in my opinion, climate change is bad. The right move is to write, climate change causes three measurable shifts in coastal sea level. No I think. No should. Just what is.

Game plan. First, three organizational structures every expository essay uses — compare and contrast, cause and effect, and problem and solution. Second, the transition words that hold each structure together. Third, the formal, objective tone that makes a reader trust the writing. Two pauses, on real assignment-style paragraphs.

Expository writing has three workhorse structures. Compare and contrast — show how two things are alike and different. Cause and effect — show what made something happen, and what happened next. Problem and solution — name a real problem, explain why it matters, and propose a workable fix. Every expository paragraph in this module uses exactly one of these shapes.

Compare and contrast has two layouts. Block — you describe all of subject A, then all of subject B. Point by point — you pick one feature, compare both subjects on it, then move to the next feature. Block is easier to write. Point by point is easier for a reader to follow. Use point by point when the features are what matter most.

Cause and effect builds a chain. Name the cause. Name the immediate effect. Then name the longer-term effect that follows. Strong cause and effect writing uses the word because — and earns it with evidence. Weak writing uses the word so, then jumps to a conclusion without showing the link. The transitions therefore, as a result, and consequently signal the chain cleanly.

Problem and solution writing does three jobs in order. State the problem clearly and explain who it affects. Show evidence that the problem is real — numbers, examples, sources. Propose a specific, workable solution and explain why it would help. The mistake is jumping straight to your solution. A reader who has not seen the problem cannot judge the fix.

Your turn. You see three writing prompts on the slide — one about two novels you read this year, one about why a local river is losing fish, and one about long lunch lines in the cafeteria. Pause now. For each prompt, name the expository structure that fits best, and write one topic sentence using that structure. Press play when you are ready.

Time. A strong response sounds like this. Two novels — compare and contrast. Topic sentence — although both novels follow a young protagonist, their endings differ sharply in tone. Local river losing fish — cause and effect. Topic sentence — three causes, working together, explain the steady decline of fish populations in the river. Cafeteria lunch lines — problem and solution. Topic sentence — long lunch lines cost students nearly half their meal time, but a staggered release schedule would cut the wait by more than half.

Two tools hold expository writing together. Transitions tell a reader how sentences relate — similarly, in contrast, because of, as a result, however, for example. Use them at the start of sentences and between paragraphs. Tone keeps the writing credible. Formal — no slang, no contractions, no I or you. Objective — no opinion words like best, terrible, or amazing. Just what is true, in the writer's calmest voice.

One more. The slide shows a rough paragraph about cafeteria lunch lines. It has the right idea but the wrong tone — and it is missing transitions. Pause now. Rewrite it as one tight expository paragraph. Add three transition words and underline them. Replace any slang or opinion words with formal, objective language. Press play when you are ready.

Time. A strong rewrite sounds like this. The cafeteria currently releases all four grade levels at once, which creates a single line of nearly six hundred students. As a result, students at the back wait up to twelve minutes before sitting down. Consequently, many stop eating full meals. A staggered release, however, would cut peak line length by more than half. Three underlined transitions. No slang. No opinion words. Just the chain of facts and one workable fix.

Here are the three paragraphs you are about to write — one hundred and fifty words each. Paragraph one — compare and contrast two books, films, or historical events you have studied. Paragraph two — cause and effect of a social or natural phenomenon. Paragraph three — a problem and solution for an issue at school or in your community. Each paragraph must use at least three appropriate transition words. Underline every transition you use. If a paragraph has no underlines, the structure is hiding.

Recap. Three structures — compare and contrast, cause and effect, problem and solution. One paragraph picks one structure. Transitions tell the reader how the sentences connect. Tone stays formal and objective — no slang, no opinion words, no I think. Underline every transition before you submit. No underlines means no structure.

How to actually master this. Read the Purdue Online Writing Lab pages on expository essays and on transition words — they show the full menu of transitions sorted by purpose. Watch the Khan Academy lesson on compare and contrast essay structure. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the three expository paragraphs — one of each structure, one hundred and fifty words each, three transitions underlined per paragraph. Book an advisor check-in if your paragraphs keep slipping into opinion. Next up — your English One end-of-course portfolio review.
